opportunity_overview.title: VRS AI Chatbot Solution to Enhance Agency Recruitment Efforts
opportunity_overview.agency: Peace Corps — Office of Volunteer Recruitment and Service (VRS)
opportunity_overview.office: Interior Business Center (IBC), Acquisition Services Directorate (AQD), 381 Elden Street, Suite 2000A, Herndon VA 20170
opportunity_overview.posted_date: PWS dated March 16, 2026; Amendment 1 effective April 3, 2026
opportunity_overview.response_deadline: Not specified in the provided documents — contracting officer is Michael McGuire; submit via email per Section 4 of RFQ
source_documents: RFQ 140D0426Q0324 Amendment 1 — Contractor Questions and Government Responses (Q&A document, 33 pages)
RFQ Contract Amendment — Standard Form 30, Amendment/Modification No. 0001, effective 04/03/2026
Performance Work Statement (PWS) — Peace Corps VRS AI Chatbot Solution, dated March 16, 2026 (27 pages, Attachment 1 to RFQ)

contract_details.contract_type: Firm-Fixed-Price (FFP) Purchase Order; pricing structured to include: (1) implementation/RAG training labor, (2) Peace Corps administrator/user training and materials, (3) year-over-year recurring costs (usage, hosting, subscription, Tier 3 support); each of five performance periods priced separately; SaaS licensing costs treated as ODCs excluded from FAR 52.219-14 subcontracting limitation calculation
contract_details.set_aside: Total Small Business (only small businesses eligible; FAR 52.219-14 Limitations on Subcontracting applies; prime must perform >50% of labor; non-certified firms at time of quote submission not eligible)
contract_details.estimated_value: Not publicly disclosed; Government confirmed an IGCE exists for both the base year and full period of performance but will not release figures
contract_details.period_of_performance: Base Period: June 1, 2026 – May 31, 2027; Option Period 1: June 1, 2027 – May 31, 2028; Option Period 2: June 1, 2028 – May 31, 2029; Option Period 3: June 1, 2029 – May 31, 2030; Option Period 4: June 1, 2030 – May 31, 2031; total not to exceed five (5) years if all options exercised; continuation contingent on fund availability and acceptable contractor performance
contract_details.place_of_performance: Contractor site within the United States of America (all work off-site/remote); no offshore or nearshore resources permitted; Peace Corps website (peacecorps.gov) is the deployment target; no on-site contractor presence required

requirements_summary.scope_overview: The Peace Corps seeks a small business contractor to implement, integrate, and maintain a FedRAMP High authorized, commercially available SaaS conversational AI chatbot on peacecorps.gov to enhance volunteer recruitment, automate public inquiry handling, support multilingual engagement, and seamlessly escalate interactions to live Peace Corps agents.
key_requirements: FedRAMP High authorized SaaS conversational AI solution — must be authorized or actively in-process for FedRAMP High designation at time of quote submission; FedRAMP Moderate-only solutions will NOT be considered; solution includes chatbot application and all platform infrastructure; hybrid/segmented architectures acceptable if all components meet FedRAMP High; offeror may serve as system integrator deploying a third-party FedRAMP High authorized platform

Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG): AI responses must be grounded in Peace Corps-curated dataset only (est. 1,000–10,000 pages at initial deployment); Peace Corps provides all source content as GFI; contractor provides RAG structure and framework; hierarchical data ranking required; after deployment, Peace Corps staff manage and update the RAG independently

Website integration: Chatbot must be seamlessly embedded on peacecorps.gov (currently Django 5/Wagtail 8 CMS); solution must be CMS-agnostic; Peace Corps will whitelist script-src in content security policy (CSP); no direct CRM integration required at initial deployment

Multilingual support: English and Spanish required at initial deployment; additional languages evaluated as Best Value differentiator; single English-language RAG dataset; contractor responsible for translation quality including 'Golden Glossaries' and safety/medical protocol accuracy

Live agent escalation and queue management: contractor provides complete queue management logic; Peace Corps has a 10-contractor internal team (Office of Recruitment) as Tier 1 escalation; live agents available 15 hours/day; no existing live-agent platform — contractor must provide the interface; robust queue management including real-time position tracking, estimated wait times, delay notifications, callback capabilities, load balancing among agents

Continuous learning with human-in-the-loop: real-time capture of user interactions for analysis, feedback loops, human-approved knowledge base updates; Peace Corps OCIO Product Managers and IT Specialists must be able to manage ongoing administration independently post-deployment
99.9% uptime SLA during business hours
Section 508 compliance (VPAT acceptable for demonstration); WCAG 2.1 standards implied
FIPS 140-2 compliant encryption in transit and at rest

HIPAA-sensitive interaction handling: chatbot must detect and redirect PHI/PII inputs to qualified Peace Corps staff; full BAA NOT required; HIPAA context primarily involves medical clearance applicants and unsolicited PHI from members of the public
No CCPA compliance required (removed from PWS per Q&A response #69)
AI Risk Management: contractor must describe their AI risk management process (no specific framework mandated, but NIST AI RMF is an implied reference)
Transparency and explainability: documented decision-making processes; source material citation for users; root cause analysis capability for incorrect responses
Data governance: all data owned by Government; CSV format required for data export; secure data segmentation, masking, redaction tools; user consent required; data minimization principles apply
Analytics/KPI dashboard: live dashboard monitoring user satisfaction, response accuracy, adoption rates, containment rate, escalation rate, top user intents; first delivery within 15 days of go-live
Emergency response protocols: detect self-harm or crisis language; route to appropriate resources
Fair and non-discriminatory operation: Title VI compliance; regular bias audits
Scalability to accommodate increasing user volumes over the five-year PoP
All work performed within the United States; no offshore resources

Subcontracting allowed; SaaS/commercial software license costs excluded from FAR 52.219-14 50% limitation calculation and classified as ODCs; compliance period measured by end of base term and each subsequent option period

Personnel security: all contractor and subcontractor personnel require favorable background investigation adjudication (Tier 1 minimum for network access; Tier 2 or higher for PII/CUI access); NDAs required for all contractor/subcontractor employees
Kaspersky Lab and FAR 52.204-25 Section 889 telecommunications ban compliance required
Exit strategy: complete machine-readable data export in non-proprietary format (JSON or CSV) upon contract termination
requirements_summary.clearance_requirements: No national security clearance specified; however, all contractor and subcontractor personnel must have favorable adjudication of background investigations — Tier 1 or higher before Peace Corps network/IT system access; Tier 2 or higher before access to PII/CUI; Tier 2 or higher before privileged access to Peace Corps network or IT systems; Peace Corps sponsors investigations when deemed necessary
requirements_summary.key_personnel: No specific named labor categories or FTE counts mandated by the Government; offerors must propose their own staffing approach and level of effort; at minimum, the solution must be administrable post-deployment by Peace Corps OCIO Product Managers and IT Specialists without contractor dependency; Government confirmed key personnel past performance (not just corporate) may be considered for Factor B evaluation for emerging small businesses
deliverables: Post-award kickoff meeting (virtual, within 10 business days of start of performance)
Draft QASP with quote submission (Volume IV); final QASP within 30 days after start of performance
Project Plan — initial within 15 days after start of performance; maintained throughout PoP
Data Protection Plan — within 15 days of start of PoP; updated as necessary
Requirements Specification Document — within 30 days after kickoff meeting (requires agency approval before design phase)
Design and Architecture Documentation — within 45 days following approval of Requirements Specification Document
Integration Plan and Report — report within 15 days of successful integration
Implementation Plan — submitted 45 days before scheduled implementation date
Test Plan — submitted 30 days prior to scheduled start of testing
Usability Testing Report — within 15 days after final prototype delivery
User Acceptance Testing (UAT) — completed within 30 days following delivery of final design document; results within 10 days after UAT completion
Training Materials and Knowledge Transfer (user manuals, guides, video tutorials) — delivered 10 days before go-live; training session for agency staff
Final Chatbot Solution — deployed within 60 days of successful completion of documentation, testing, cybersecurity assessment phases, and issuance of Peace Corps ATO
KPI Dashboard — first delivery within 15 days of go-live; regular updates per agency specification
Monthly Status Reports — by 10th of each month with invoice, until go-live
Meeting Minutes — within 5 business days after each meeting
Final Status Report — within 30 calendar days from go-live
Subcontracting Report (Attachment 2) — submitted to Contracting Officer 60 days prior to end of base period and all option periods
Monthly vulnerability scan reports (OS, database, web application) and POA&M updates
Monthly personnel separated staff report (beginning 60 days after execution of each option period)
Transparency and accountability documentation — maintained throughout PoP
Privacy Threshold Assessment (PTA) — contractor assists in preparation
Complete machine-readable data export in JSON/CSV format upon contract termination

evaluation_criteria.evaluation_method: Best Value Tradeoff (not LPTA); Government confirmed in Q&A #39 and #75; price reasonableness determination will be conducted (price realism analysis NOT confirmed per Q&A #110)
factors: Factor A — Technical Approach (referenced in RFQ Section 5, pages 20-21; Volume I, 20-page limit excluding cover page and table of contents)

Factor B — Past Performance (Volume II, no page limit; three references required, within last 3 years, similar in complexity and magnitude; commercial, SLED, federal, and subcontractor references acceptable; key personnel past performance considered for emerging small businesses; prime contractor past performance preferred)
Factor C — Assumptions, Conditions, or Exceptions (Volume III, no page limit)
Factor D — Draft Quality Assurance Surveillance Plan (Volume IV, no page limit; contractor format acceptable; serves as starting point for post-award QASP)

Factor E — Price (Volume V, no page limit; FFP by period; structured as implementation labor, training, and recurring year-over-year costs; priced separately for each of five periods; timeline to go-live evaluated as Best Value differentiator; additional language support beyond English/Spanish evaluated as Best Value differentiator; presentable reporting formats such as Word/PDF considered in Best Value determination)
evaluation_criteria.submission_requirements: Five separate volumes submitted electronically via email; all five volumes encouraged in a single email (separate emails acceptable if file size prevents consolidation); PDF and Word formats acceptable; Volume I — Technical (20-page limit, cover page and table of contents excluded from page count); Volume II — Past Performance (no page limit); Volume III — Assumptions, Conditions, or Exceptions (no page limit); Volume IV — Draft QASP (no page limit); Volume V — Price (no page limit; FFP for base period and each option period separately; structure to include: implementation/RAG training, Peace Corps staff training/materials, year-over-year recurring costs); cover letter may be included for each volume; SF 1449 is for internal reference only and need NOT be submitted
evaluation_criteria.notes: Offerors are responsible for ensuring complete quote is received and properly identified; incomplete quotes may not be considered; contractors may use existing GSA/contract labor categories and rates as basis for pricing with discounts encouraged; SaaS licensing costs classified as ODCs and excluded from FAR 52.219-14 subcontracting calculation; pricing must account for variable usage-based AI/cloud costs built into FFP; Government will accept pricing structured as: (1) implementation, (2) training/materials, (3) year-over-year recurring costs; go-live timeline included in technical response and evaluated as Best Value factor

competitive_intelligence.incumbent: None — this is a new requirement with no current spend and no incumbent contractor (confirmed in Q&A #8, #106, #138)
competitive_intelligence.estimated_competition: Set-aside for small businesses only; FedRAMP High authorization requirement (or active in-process designation) is a significant barrier to entry, substantially limiting the pool of eligible offerors; no platform preference stated; hybrid/segmented architectures and third-party platform integrations acceptable, broadening the competitive field somewhat; subcontracting and teaming allowed with prime performing >50% of labor; commercial AI firms with federal validation (DoD SBIR Phase II) and adjacent commercial deployments are competitive; single award
notable_terms: FedRAMP High is an absolute requirement — FedRAMP Moderate solutions will NOT be considered; solution must be in-process at minimum at time of quote submission; FedRAMP 20x is NOT acceptable (not scheduled for High impact pilot until FY27)

CCPA compliance requirement has been REMOVED from PWS per Q&A #69 — NOTE: PWS Section 3.1.13 as written still references CCPA; government stated it will be corrected but the provided PWS document has NOT been updated

CROSS-DOCUMENT DISCREPANCY: PWS Section 6.1 references FedRAMP Moderate impact systems; all other references (PWS 3.1.1, RFQ Section 1.3, Q&A responses) require FedRAMP High; government confirmed PWS will be corrected but the provided PWS text has not been fully updated
No BAA required despite HIPAA-sensitive interaction handling being a firm requirement
All work must be performed within the United States — no offshore or nearshore resources permitted

ATO process: Peace Corps will issue its own Agency ATO leveraging contractor's FedRAMP package; contractor may begin implementation planning and RAG development during ATO review but cannot go to production until ATO is complete

ODCs initially stated as not anticipated in Section 1.10 but later revised to remove this limitation; SaaS licensing costs are ODCs excluded from FAR 52.219-14 calculation — NOTE: if Azure is proposed, Government confirmed Azure costs remain with the Agency (Q&A #118)
Government owns all data; CSV format required for data export; all interaction data may be used for system improvement
FAR 52.219-14 Limitations on Subcontracting compliance measured by end of base term and each subsequent option period (not per order)
Response time SLA defined qualitatively ('fast enough to ensure smooth and natural conversation flow') — no specific millisecond/second threshold stated
Chatbot scope covers entire volunteer lifecycle from lead to returned volunteer — not limited to front-end lead generation

Peace Corps' existing tech stack: AWS and Azure for cloud, Dynamics CRM (Click/Dimensions, upgrading from 8.2 to 9.1), SQL and Oracle databases, Power Platform; Django 5/Wagtail 8 CMS; no existing live-agent or case management system
Over 90% of current public inquiries are predictable and from a limited set of subjects — strong RAG-anchored solution should perform well
If Azure is proposed by the vendor, Peace Corps confirmed Azure use costs remain with the Agency
No specific AI risk management framework mandated (NIST AI RMF referenced implicitly)

bd_action_items.pursuit_recommendation: Best positioned: a small business (or small business prime with large business subcontractors for the FedRAMP High platform) with demonstrated experience deploying FedRAMP High or in-process conversational AI/chatbot solutions in federal or regulated environments, preferably with RAG-based architectures; firms with existing relationships with FedRAMP High authorized SaaS AI platforms (e.g., as a system integrator) and prior federal agency AI deployment experience will have a strong advantage; key differentiators will be go-live timeline, multilingual capability depth beyond English/Spanish, and analytics/KPI maturity; emerging small businesses with strong key personnel records can compete on Past Performance

risks_and_flags: RESPONSE DEADLINE NOT PROVIDED: The submission deadline is not specified in any of the three provided documents — this is a critical gap; offerors must check SAM.gov for the current response deadline

FedRAMP HIGH BARRIER: Only solutions with active FedRAMP High authorization OR actively in-process at time of quote submission are eligible; this is a go/no-go qualifier that will significantly narrow the competitive field; firms must validate their platform partner's FedRAMP status before investing in a response

UNDEFINED VOLUME RISK IN FFP PRICING: Government acknowledges engagement will 'dramatically increase' but provides no ceiling on interactions; all usage-based AI compute costs must be absorbed in FFP pricing; this represents potentially unlimited financial exposure for the contractor — firms should carefully model worst-case usage scenarios and consider risk mitigation language

ATO TIMELINE UNCERTAINTY: Peace Corps ATO timeline is TBD and will be based on final project plan; no interim operating capability is permitted until ATO is issued; this creates schedule risk within the base period (June 1, 2026 – May 31, 2027) and could push go-live into Option Period 1

AZURE COST TREATMENT: If a vendor proposes Azure, the Government confirmed Azure costs remain with the Agency — this creates a pricing asymmetry between Azure-based and non-Azure-based solutions that offerors must account for in their pricing structure

CCPA REMOVAL NOT REFLECTED IN PWS: Q&A #69 states CCPA was removed from the PWS, but the provided PWS document (Section 3.1.13) still includes CCPA as a requirement — offerors should acknowledge the amendment removes this requirement but should not rely solely on the Q&A; request final amended PWS from the contracting officer

FEDRAMP MODERATE LANGUAGE IN PWS NOT FULLY CORRECTED: PWS Section 6.1 references 'Moderate' impact systems; Q&A responses #25, #64, #87, #90 confirm this is an error and the correct requirement is FedRAMP High; government stated the PWS 'will be corrected' but the provided PWS text has not been updated — this is an unresolved document inconsistency

SUBCONTRACTING 50% CALCULATION AMBIGUITY: SaaS licensing/ODC costs are excluded from the FAR 52.219-14 calculation, which could make it easier to comply; however, if the majority of contract value is SaaS licensing, the remaining labor value split must be tracked carefully

NO LIVE-AGENT SYSTEM EXISTS: Contractor must build queue management and escalation logic from scratch with no existing platform integration point — this is a significant scope item often underestimated in pricing

PRICING MODEL MISMATCH: RFQ requires FFP but the underlying cost drivers are usage-based SaaS — firms must carefully construct FFP pricing that accounts for variable AI compute costs, potentially over multiple years of traffic growth

HIPAA HANDLING WITHOUT BAA: Government confirmed no BAA is required, yet PHI/PII from applicants may be entered into the chatbot — offerors should address this in their legal/compliance approach as it may create liability questions
SINGLE AWARD: Only one vendor will be selected — a strong, differentiated technical approach is essential
cross_document_discrepancies: FEDRAMP AUTHORIZATION LEVEL CONFLICT: PWS Section 3.1.1 and RFQ Section 1.3 require 'FedRAMP High Authorization'; PWS Section 6.1 references 'FedRAMP Continuous Monitoring Requirements for Moderate impact systems (as defined in FIPS PUB 199).' The Q&A document (responses #25, #64, #87, #90, #147) consistently confirms FedRAMP HIGH is required and states the PWS will be corrected — however, the provided PWS text has NOT been updated to remove the 'Moderate' language in Section 6.1. This is the most significant cross-document discrepancy in the package.

CCPA REQUIREMENT CONFLICT: PWS Section 3.1.13 as provided includes California Consumer Privacy Act (CCPA) compliance as a requirement. Q&A response #69 states 'This has been removed from the PWS.' The provided PWS document still contains the CCPA requirement. Offerors should not propose CCPA compliance as a PWS requirement but should note the inconsistency.

ODC TREATMENT CONFLICT: RFQ Section 1.10 reportedly states 'Other Direct Costs (ODC) are not anticipated in performance of this order.' However, Q&A #118 states 'This section has been revised to remove ODCs' and Q&A responses #102, #122, #149 all direct SaaS licensing costs to be included as ODCs. The solicitation as provided appears to contain a now-superseded ODC restriction that was revised by Amendment 1.

DELIVERABLE DESCRIPTION MINOR VARIANCE: PWS Section 3.4.1 states the final solution 'must be delivered within 60 days of the successful completion of the final document documentation, testing and cybersecurity risk management and assessment phases'; PWS Section 4.2.14 adds 'and issuance of an Authority to Operate (ATO) by the Peace Corps' as an additional condition. Section 4.2.14 is the more complete and operationally accurate statement.

PERFORMANCE STANDARD TABLE (PWS Section 5.0): References 'The Contractor provided Hardware and Software Configuration' as the performance standard — this language appears to be boilerplate/placeholder text that does not accurately describe a SaaS/AI chatbot solution and may be a carryover from a prior template.

sections.title: Volume I — Technical Approach (20-page limit, cover page and TOC excluded)
requirements_addressed: PWS 3.1.1 — FedRAMP High Authorization: describe the solution's FedRAMP status, authorization boundary, inherited controls, and path to Peace Corps ATO
PWS 3.1.3 — CMS integration approach for peacecorps.gov (Django 5/Wagtail 8 agnostic)
PWS 3.1.4 — RAG architecture, hierarchical data ranking, hallucination prevention, dataset structure
PWS 3.1.5 — Continuous learning mechanisms, human-in-the-loop model updates, enabling Peace Corps staff to manage independently
PWS 3.1.6 — Optimization methodology, response time approach, computational cost balancing
PWS 3.1.9 — Multilingual support (English/Spanish minimum; additional languages as differentiator); translation quality approach including Golden Glossaries
PWS 3.1.11 — Live agent escalation, queue management logic, callback capabilities, load balancing
PWS 3.1.12 — Scalability architecture for dramatically increasing user volumes
PWS 3.1.17/3.1.18/3.1.19 — Analytics, KPI dashboard, usage reporting capabilities
PWS 3.1.20/3.1.21 — AI risk management approach, explainability, root cause analysis for incorrect responses
PWS 3.1.22 — Data governance, segmentation, masking, redaction, consent management
PWS 3.1.23 — HIPAA detection and redirect logic
PWS 3.2 through 3.4 — Post-award implementation approach and go-live timeline (evaluated as Best Value factor)
PWS 6.1 — Security and FedRAMP compliance approach, continuous monitoring plan
PWS 6.2 — Section 508/WCAG accessibility approach (VPAT)
Q&A #52/#58 — Proposed go-live timeline (evaluated in Best Value determination)
sections.guidance: Lead with the FedRAMP High authorization status of the proposed solution (active or in-process with documentation), demonstrate a concrete and credible go-live timeline within the base year, and clearly articulate how the RAG architecture prevents hallucinations while enabling independent Peace Corps staff management post-deployment.

sections.title: Volume II — Past Performance
requirements_addressed: RFQ Section 5, Factor B — three references, within last 3 years, similar in complexity and magnitude to PWS requirements
Q&A #3 — Key personnel past performance accepted for emerging small businesses
Q&A #105/#150 — Subcontractor past performance accepted; prime performance preferred
Q&A #134 — Commercial AI platform deployments in adjacent domains (talent acquisition, conversational AI) considered relevant
Q&A #136 — Commercial and SLED references acceptable if recent and relevant
sections.guidance: Prioritize references demonstrating FedRAMP-compliant AI/chatbot deployments for federal agencies or highly regulated sectors, emphasizing RAG implementation, live-agent integration, and measurable outcomes such as inquiry deflection rates, uptime, and user adoption.

sections.title: Volume III — Assumptions, Conditions, or Exceptions
requirements_addressed: RFQ Section 4 — submitted under separate cover per solicitation instructions
Any assumptions made regarding undefined usage volumes, ATO timeline, or integration dependencies
Any exceptions to PWS requirements (note: CCPA removal and FedRAMP Moderate correction should be acknowledged here)
sections.guidance: Keep exceptions minimal and focused on clarifications (e.g., Azure cost treatment, ATO timeline dependency, RAG content delivery schedule); use this volume to proactively address the FedRAMP High/Moderate discrepancy and CCPA removal to demonstrate thorough solicitation review.

sections.title: Volume IV — Draft Quality Assurance Surveillance Plan (QASP)
requirements_addressed: PWS 3.2.1 — QASP covering all task areas and deliverables per PWS
Q&A #68 — Pre-award draft QASP serves as starting point for post-award QASP
Q&A #103 — Contractor format acceptable; must cover all required performance objectives
FAR Overhaul 46.4 — basis for QASP development
PWS Section 5.0 — Performance Requirements Summary (95% on-time delivery threshold)
sections.guidance: Structure the QASP around measurable performance thresholds tied directly to PWS deliverable timelines, the 99.9% uptime SLA, chatbot response accuracy metrics, and escalation containment rates to demonstrate a rigorous quality management framework.

sections.title: Volume V — Price/Cost
requirements_addressed: RFQ Section 4.3/4.4 — FFP price quote for base period and each of four option periods separately

Q&A #132 — Structure as: (1) implementation/RAG training labor, (2) Peace Corps staff training and training materials, (3) year-over-year recurring costs (usage, hosting, subscription, Tier 3 support)
Q&A #102/#122/#149 — SaaS licensing costs included as ODCs, excluded from FAR 52.219-14 50% calculation
Q&A #109 — Existing GSA/contract labor categories and rates may be used as basis with discounts
Q&A #123 — Account for dramatic but undefined traffic growth in FFP model
Q&A #35 — CSV data export format; presentable report formats (Word/PDF) considered in Best Value
sections.guidance: Clearly separate implementation labor, training costs, and year-over-year recurring SaaS/hosting/support costs across all five periods; explicitly document assumptions about usage volume growth and any usage caps or pricing tiers built into the FFP to demonstrate transparency and price reasonableness.

win_themes: FedRAMP High Readiness and Security-First Architecture: Demonstrate an active or well-documented in-process FedRAMP High authorization that directly addresses the Government's stated concern about PHI/PII exposure from unsolicited user inputs, with a clear, accelerated path to Peace Corps ATO within the base year.

Operational Independence Through Human-Centered AI Design: Emphasize that Peace Corps staff — not the contractor — will own and drive continuous improvement of the chatbot post-deployment, through an intuitive RAG management interface, comprehensive training, and Tier 3 support that empowers the Agency's OCIO team rather than creating long-term vendor dependency.

Proven Recruitment Intelligence at Scale: Demonstrate measurable outcomes from analogous conversational AI deployments (lead conversion, inquiry deflection, multilingual engagement) and present a credible, phased go-live timeline that gets the chatbot live as early as possible in the base period, directly addressing Peace Corps' urgency to reduce staff workload and increase volunteer pipeline conversion.
